---
description: "Applies the i18n-translator agent to localize a specific frontend page based on its URL."
---

@i18n-translator Please localize the React component associated with this frontend URL: {{url}}.

1. Identify the exact '.tsx' file that serves this route (e.g. from 'pages/').
2. Follow your 'i18n-translator' core directives to extract all hardcoded English UI text displayed to the user.
3. **MANDATORY**: Physically write/overwrite the '.tsx' file using file editing tools or a Node.js script. Do NOT just output the rewritten code in the chat. You MUST modify the actual file on disk. 
4. **MANDATORY**: Physically append the newly extracted strings to BOTH 'public/locales/en/translation.json' and 'public/locales/my/translation.json' on disk using a script. Make sure you use the EXACT English string from the UI as the JSON key AND value for the English file. NEVER invent nested or snake_case key paths (e.g. use "Students Directory": "...", DO NOT use "admin_students.title": "...").
CRITICAL WARNING: When writing to `my/translation.json` or `en/translation.json`, you MUST ensure the resulting files are valid JSON. Be careful not to append literal string newline characters like `\n` or extraneous bytes at the end of the file which will corrupt the JSON and cause react-i18next fails to load (thus leaving the UI blank or untranslated). Always use `JSON.parse` to validate the written JSON files.
CRITICAL WARNING: When writing to `my/translation.json`, you MUST properly translate the values into Myanmar (Burmese). DO NOT simply duplicate the English values into the Myanmar locale file. Failure to actually translate the strings will cause the UI to remain in English even when the language toggles.
CRITICAL WARNING: Due to Windows PowerShell encoding limitations, NEVER use inline PowerShell or `node -e "..."` via the `run_in_terminal` tool to write or paste Unicode characters (like Burmese translations), as this will corrupt the output into `?????`. You MUST use the `create_file` tool to save a valid `.cjs` script containing the actual strings, and then execute it with `node` to apply the translations to disk.
5. Inject 'import { useTranslation } from "react-i18next";' and replace the original strings with 't(...)' bindings. You MUST use the exact string expressions as keys (e.g. `t("Students Directory")`). NEVER invent nested dot-notation strings.
CRITICAL WARNING: When using the `t` function from `useTranslation()`, you MUST ensure that no local variables or array `map` parameters are also named `t`. You must rename any conflicting variables (e.g. change `TYPES.map(t => ...)` to `TYPES.map(typeOption => ...)`) to avoid variable shadowing, which will cause a fatal runtime crash.
CRITICAL WARNING: When doing mass string replacements (e.g., via script or Regex) to add `t(...)` wrappers or rename shadowing variables like `t`, use STRICT word boundaries (`\b`) to avoid accidentally corrupting other unrelated words that contain the letter 't' (such as replacing `draft.title` into `draftoast.title` or `draf.title`). Verify your replacements to ensure no unintended strings were mangled.
CRITICAL WARNING: NEVER call the `t()` translation function inside utility/helper functions declared outside the React component. If a utility function needs to translate text, you MUST explicitly pass `t` as an argument from the component to the utility (e.g., `formatCountdown(ms, t)`). Calling `t()` improperly outside a component's scope will result in a fatal React crash (White Screen).
CRITICAL WARNING: You MUST ensure that the `import { useTranslation } from 'react-i18next';` statement is successfully added to the top of the file. Be careful with replacing exact import strings, as the file might use single quotes (`'`) instead of double quotes (`"`). Double-check the file after your edit to ensure the import is present, otherwise the application will crash with a ReferenceError.
6. Verify the files are actually updated.

Here is the URL to process: {{url}}
